title: First Echo: Altitude Is Privilege
dramaticQuestion: What remains of air power when altitude, information, and expensive machinery are progressively stripped away?

perspective.actorRole: F-22 pilot defending a pressured air corridor

sorties.playerFantasy: Begin above the weather in an exceptionally capable fighter and dismantle a drone raid before it reaches the defended corridor.
sorties.scenarioSummary: The campaign opens with apparently abundant speed, altitude, sensor support, and ammunition, but the raid presents more targets than the player can engage carelessly and every high-energy correction consumes position and time.
sorties.centralDecision: Prioritize targets and control closure instead of treating superior performance as unlimited time or perfect information.
sorties.failureMeaning: The corridor takes damage and the run absorbs a visible readiness cost, while the historical context and first coaching fragment remain discoverable.
sorties.successMeaning: The raid is contained, the F-22 force remains available, and the player earns the first historical sortie that gives the corridor a human past.

perspective.actorRole: F-86 pilot near the Yalu with limited fuel and incomplete identification

sorties.playerFantasy: Fly the same geometry without modern abundance: visually acquire, identify, fight, and still preserve enough energy and fuel to return.
sorties.scenarioSummary: A damaged opponent runs toward the sanctuary line while fuel falls and identification remains imperfect. The aircraft gives honest performance but little excess capacity to repair a poor approach.
sorties.centralDecision: Decide whether a fleeting tactical opportunity justifies spending fuel, certainty, and political margin near the border.
sorties.failureMeaning: Failure reveals whether the decisive error was identification, closure, energy, fuel planning, or an unjustified border risk rather than reducing the outcome to a kill count.
sorties.successMeaning: The player returns with a defensible decision and recognizes that the modern corridor inherited geographic and political limits rather than appearing as an abstract arena.

perspective.actorRole: Experienced remote drone operator flying a limited fixed-wing aircraft

sorties.playerFantasy: Survive beneath the radar picture in a slow, energy-limited drone by reading terrain and protecting a fragile control link.
sorties.scenarioSummary: With high-end aircraft scarce and the network contested, the operator follows a river-and-road corridor at low altitude. Weak acceleration, narrow sensor coverage, link geometry, obstacles, and uncertain tasking make terrain knowledge more valuable than nominal technology.
sorties.centralDecision: Choose when to remain masked, when to climb for information or link quality, and when the mission is no longer worth the aircraft and recovered data.
sorties.failureMeaning: A crash, lost link, or unjustified exposure identifies a specific terrain, energy, or communications decision and carries a bounded readiness consequence.
sorties.successMeaning: The operator completes or responsibly aborts the task, and the player understands the opposing force as experienced people adapting under scarcity rather than an anonymous drone supply.

nestedMemory.ownerActor: The DPRK operator flying this sortie
nestedMemory.location: Ukraine, exact location withheld pending research
nestedMemory.epistemicLimit: The sequence is a subjective character memory and cannot establish the complete history, prevalence, or institutional meaning of DPRK experience in Ukraine.
nestedMemory.laterConsequence: A remembered choice about terrain masking and a damaged link explains the operator's Korean route choice and affects whether another character trusts the abort.

echoes.constant: Fixed-gun geometry still requires lead, range judgment, closure control, and enough energy to leave.
echoes.changed: The F-22 begins with greater performance and information; the F-86 makes each correction and identification decision visibly expensive.
echoes.dramaticPurpose: Reveal that superior machinery can conceal but cannot repeal the physical problem the historical pilot had to understand directly.

echoes.constant: Altitude trades concealment for energy, sensor horizon, communications reach, and exposure.
echoes.changed: The F-22 can usually choose altitude; the attritable drone must bargain for seconds above terrain masking.
echoes.dramaticPurpose: Turn the opening power fantasy into a remembered privilege when attrition later forces the player into limited drones.

echoes.constant: A river valley is simultaneously geography, flight path, communications geometry, and political boundary.
echoes.changed: The historical pilot sees terrain and a line on a map; the modern operator receives denser but fallible geofences, tracks, and tasking.
echoes.dramaticPurpose: Make the modern route carry remembered political and human meaning rather than function as a convenient low-poly trench.

carriedKnowledge: Closure control learned in the opening is made explicit by the F-86 and tested again in a low-performance drone.
The player learns that more symbols can still represent uncertain claims.
The player recognizes altitude as an operational resource rather than a neutral camera position.
The same terrain can preserve physical continuity while institutions, vegetation, infrastructure, and memory change.
authoredInvariants: The F-22 sortie is the campaign's first playable mission.
The first F-86 context is revealed before the low-level modern return.
The Ukraine sequence remains an owned memory inside the 2030s storyline.
No outcome narration declares the fictional 2034 conflict a forecast.
authoredDifferences: Weather, raid composition, serviceability, and optional objectives may vary within validated bounds.
The low-level route may choose among authored corridors that preserve the same visibility, link, and terrain decisions.
The player may reach the drone sortie because of F-22 attrition, mission tasking, or a perspective chapter, but the transition cause must be explicit.

learningObjectives.playerCan: Control closure before gun range and preserve a viable separation or recovery path after the pass.
evaluatedBy: peak closure after offensive geometry is established
energy and fuel remaining after disengagement

learningObjectives.playerCan: Delay or bound an action when track age, source, identity, or authorization does not support certainty.
evaluatedBy: track age and source at the decision event
time between contradictory observations and action
whether the player used visual or route evidence to revise the estimate

learningObjectives.playerCan: Choose altitude by balancing terrain clearance, energy, visibility, sensor horizon, link geometry, and threat exposure.
evaluatedBy: radar altitude and vertical-speed history near terrain
link margin before and after climbs
time exposed above the authored masking height

triggerEvidence: excess closure inside the declared gun gate
insufficient energy or fuel to re-engage or leave
debriefs.insight: Reconstruct the player's range, closure, lead demand, and remaining energy at the decision point; explain only the first controllable error in that chain.
neverClaims: that one target-speed number is universally correct
that the pitch assist chose the pursuit geometry
that a miss proves lack of knowledge

triggerEvidence: action on a stale or low-confidence track
a material conflict between two sources
debriefs.insight: Show the observation timeline and distinguish world truth from what the actor could reasonably believe when the decision was made.
neverClaims: that the tactical display was omniscient
that the player should use hidden simulation truth

triggerEvidence: terrain clearance below the mission margin
a climb that restores the link but creates threat exposure
an attempted climb without enough energy
debriefs.insight: Replay altitude, vertical speed, energy, link margin, and threat exposure together so altitude is understood as a trade rather than a single safe value.
neverClaims: that low flight is always safer
that a lost link has one universal cause
that terrain detail not rendered to the player was fair evidence

archiveEntries.title: Energy, Closure, and the Gun Solution

claims.label: history
claims.statement: F-86 operations in northwestern Korea combined limited endurance with politically consequential restrictions around the Chinese border and Yalu sanctuary.
claims.qualification: The mission does not use disputed victory ratios or imply that every unit, date, pilot, or engagement experienced identical orders and enforcement.

claims.label: engineering
claims.statement: Producing additional lift for a harder turn increases induced drag and can spend airspeed or altitude faster than available thrust restores it.
claims.qualification: The exact energy rate is airframe-, configuration-, atmosphere-, and maneuver-dependent; the archive explains the relationship rather than promising one universal corner speed.

claims.label: engineering
claims.statement: Terrain and low altitude can obstruct or reduce line-of-sight command-and-control radio coverage, so climbing may restore a link while increasing exposure.
claims.qualification: The mission's link model is a bounded gameplay model; real propagation also depends on frequency, antennas, power, atmosphere, interference, relays, and system design.

claims.label: reconstruction
claims.statement: The playable F-22 is a public-data surrogate using declared public characteristics and gameplay-bounded systems rather than a claim to reproduce classified performance or software.
claims.qualification: The source supports only its published facts; modern combat behavior, controls, sensor fusion, and scenario effectiveness remain declared reconstruction or fiction.

claims.label: fiction
claims.statement: The 2034 Korean conflict, its initiating events, organizations, raids, readiness ledger, and operational outcomes are alternate-history fiction.
claims.qualification: Real institutions and technical constraints may anchor the setting, but the game does not present this war as a prediction.

claims.label: fiction
claims.statement: This particular DPRK operator, their Ukraine memory, its location, dialogue, actions, and later Korean consequences are invented for the campaign.
claims.qualification: Any real historical context later placed around the memory must be registered as separate history or reconstruction claims before approval.

sources.title: The U.S. Air Force in Korea
sources.publisher: U.S. Air Force Historical Support Division
sources.perspective: Official U.S. Air Force institutional history; useful but not a complete multinational or opposing account.

sources.title: Coalition Air Warfare in the Korean War, 1950–1953
sources.publisher: Air Force History and Museums Program
sources.perspective: U.S. official historical synthesis focused on coalition air operations; opposing and individual experiences require additional sources during mission research.

sources.title: Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge
sources.perspective: General aviation educational reference for aerodynamic principles, not an exact performance source for any campaign aircraft.

sources.title: Quantifying the Effects of Terrain for VHF and Higher Frequency Application
sources.publisher: National Telecommunications and Information Administration, Institute for Telecommunication Sciences
sources.perspective: General technical treatment of terrain, line-of-sight, diffraction, antenna height, and radio horizons; not a source for any fictional military link's frequency or exact performance.

sources.title: F-22 Raptor fact sheet
sources.perspective: Official public description of the aircraft; neither a complete technical dataset nor independent evidence of combat effectiveness.

abstractions.description: The F-22 flight, control, gun, and protection models are rounded public-data surrogates with explicitly bounded gameplay assistance.
abstractions.reason: Classified and unavailable data preclude an exact model, while the opening needs a coherent high-capability aircraft with honest internal physics.
abstractions.playerDisclosure: Aircraft name and archive entry both identify the public-data surrogate.

abstractions.description: The 2034 coalition and DPRK sensor, link, fusion, geofence, and authorization systems are fictional capability models built from durable functions and constraints.
abstractions.reason: Future named systems and outcomes cannot be sourced, but contacts still need age, source, uncertainty, latency, and failure behavior for gameplay.
abstractions.playerDisclosure: Archive and mission briefing label the network as 2034 fiction and expose each track's modeled evidence fields.

abstractions.description: The 1951 mission compresses geography, timing, communications, and representative constraints into one authored sortie rather than recreating a named engagement.
abstractions.reason: The lesson needs a coherent decision without falsely assigning invented acts to a real pilot or unit.
abstractions.playerDisclosure: The briefing calls the sortie a composite and the archive separates documented constraints from invented events.

abstractions.description: The Ukraine sequence is a fictional character memory whose real historical anchors remain intentionally unfilled while research and perspective review are pending.
abstractions.reason: The authoring machinery must preserve the idea without converting an unresearched contemporary-conflict vignette into canon.
abstractions.playerDisclosure: No playable release is permitted until sourced context and a fiction label can appear together.

abstractions.description: Low-level gameplay uses a limited set of high-detail authored corridors within the larger sourced terrain rather than uniform near-field density across Korea.
abstractions.reason: Motion parallax, obstacle visibility, shadows, collision truth, and stable frame time matter more than undifferentiated geographic breadth for drone flight.
abstractions.playerDisclosure: Mission boundaries follow operational route constraints and never use invisible walls inside the decision corridor.

abstractions.description: Fleet inventory, inspections, repair capacity, and parts are compressed run resources rather than a complete squadron logistics simulation.
abstractions.reason: The run needs legible attrition decisions without pretending to model every maintainer, component, supply route, and command assignment.
abstractions.playerDisclosure: Every readiness delta names its cause, duration or recovery path, and missions made unavailable.

entryConditions: A new profile receives this dossier's F-22 sortie as its first campaign node.
The historical and low-level drone sorties remain locked until their authored reveal prerequisites are met.

completionUnlocks.storyReason: Containing the modern raid reveals that the defended corridor inherited an older border problem.
The first historical chapter is learned by flying its decision rather than reading a detached unlock card.
The player's recorded opening engagement supplies evidence for the first technical debrief.
Later attrition and perspective chapters can now turn altitude privilege into a low-level route problem.

failureUnlocks.storyReason: Failure supplies especially legible closure and prioritization evidence without pretending the modern front advanced.
A damaged corridor still prompts the first fragment of its 1950s history so narrative discovery is not gated entirely by mastery.

runStateEffects.amount: outcome-derived inspection, repairable damage, or ready
runStateEffects.cause: Authoritative aircraft state and recovery outcome at sortie completion
runStateEffects.boundedRecovery: Every non-catastrophic state names a repair time or later inspection decision.

runStateEffects.cause: Only an explicit catastrophic-loss event, never an ordinary mission-grade failure
runStateEffects.boundedRecovery: Route generation retains at least one launchable non-F-22 mission and surfaces replacement or adaptation options.

runStateEffects.cause: Raid leakers damage declared corridor relays or force their displacement
runStateEffects.boundedRecovery: A repair, escort, relocation, or degraded-link route is offered before the value can reach a terminal threshold.

progression.routeGuarantee: After every outcome the director must offer at least one mission that can launch with current aircraft, network, and repair state; scarcity changes the problem but never produces a hidden unwinnable menu.

agencyRules: ROK actors set objectives, limits, and consequences rather than receiving a complete American mission list.
The 1951 pilot is a specific fictional person inside documented constraints, not an avatar for an entire coalition.
The DPRK operator has professional judgment, relationships, fear, and reasons to abort that are not reducible to obedience or fanaticism.
No side receives omniscient narration or a monopoly on competence, uncertainty, mercy, or error.
representation.civilianContext: The defended corridor, border geography, communications infrastructure, and low-level route have civilian users and consequences that briefings and outcomes must acknowledge without turning civilians into score modifiers.
perspectiveRisks: Using a current war as aesthetic backstory for an enemy character.
Implying one fictional veteran represents all DPRK personnel or a settled historical pattern.
Making the 1950s storyline exist only to validate modern coalition policy.
Treating high-end American capability as moral authority rather than temporary operational privilege.
mitigations: Keep the Ukraine memory blocked from approval until its real anchors and limitations are separately sourced.
Review the memory for Ukrainian and Korean agency as well as technical plausibility.
Give the remembered choice a later interpersonal and operational consequence rather than using it as a combat montage.
Test scripts for clear separation among character memory, institutional claim, debrief inference, and narrator fact.
representation.memoryOwnership: The Ukraine scene is explicitly the DPRK operator's incomplete memory; its framing, omissions, and emotional emphasis belong to that character and cannot settle objective history.

terrainCorridors.purpose: Establish the modern battlespace from altitude while retaining recognizable rivers, ridges, settlements, weather, and the defended corridor below.
nearFieldRequirements: No low-level proof requirement during the opening engagement.
Distant terrain must still expose relief, cloud relationship, and route-scale landmarks.

terrainCorridors.purpose: Support historically distinct surfaces over recognizable shared relief and make terrain, border, energy, and link decisions visible at low altitude.
nearFieldRequirements: Stable collision terrain and radar-altitude truth beneath the complete playable route.
Trees, buildings, poles, embankments, roads, river edges, smoke, and shadows supply motion parallax at representative drone speeds.
Thin obstacles are visible early enough to avoid or are excluded from collision authority.
1951 and 2034 vegetation, settlement, infrastructure, lighting, and damage are separate dated layers.
Terrain masking used by sensors and links matches the visible ridge and valley geometry.

visualProofs: A recorded 60-second low-level drone route maintains target frame time and has no uncommanded HUD or terrain flicker.
Independent reviewers can estimate dangerous height trend from ground cues, radar altitude, and vertical speed before warning onset.
Motion parallax remains legible in clear, snow, rain, and degraded sensor presentation variants.
The gun funnel, target data, and terrain cues remain distinguishable without occupying cockpit-scale screen area.
requiredSystems: contact belief with source, age, and uncertainty
remote-link latency, loss, and bounded lost-link behavior
accessibilityProofs: Critical altitude trend is available through redundant motion, numeric, and warning cues.
Track confidence and source are not encoded by color alone.
Debrief reconstructions support pause, scrub, text summary, and reduced-motion presentation.
Archive depth and educational prompts can be dismissed without hiding controls or objectives.
telemetryQuestions: Do players control closure more effectively in the F-86 after the F-22 debrief?
Can players explain why they climbed or stayed masked during the drone route using visible evidence?
Are low-level collisions preceded by readable cues for long enough to support a decision?
Does the historical sortie measurably change how players describe the modern corridor and border decision?
Do players perceive the DPRK operator as a character with agency rather than an equipment-delivery device?

reviews.notes: The three-sortie reveal order and echoes are authored; character identities, exact transitions, and outcome consequences remain unwritten.
evidence: braid revealOrder and echo records in this dossier

reviews.notes: The opening mission exists as a prototype contract; the historical sanctuary and low-level drone contracts require production definitions and validation cards.
evidence: sim/Doctrine/Beats.cs opening F-22 mission and capability identities

reviews.notes: Core Korea, aerodynamic, link, and F-22 anchors are registered. The 1951 composite needs narrower unit/date research and the Ukraine context is intentionally not yet claimed.
evidence: claim and source closure in this dossier

reviews.notes: Observable objectives and transfer sorties are declared; telemetry thresholds and debrief prioritization require prototype playtests.
evidence: learningObjectives and educationDelivery records in this dossier

reviews.notes: Perspective risks are explicit. The DPRK operator and Ukraine memory remain blocked pending research, character writing, and dedicated perspective review.
Low-level corridor requirements are acceptance criteria, not a claim that the current renderer satisfies them.
evidence: terrain corridor, visual proof, accessibility proof, and telemetry requirements in this dossier
